scout_targets.field_a: Cuproptosis — copper-induced cell death via FDX1-mediated Cu2+ binding to lipoylated TCA cycle proteins (DLAT, DLST, GCSH, DBT)
scout_targets.field_c: Hydrothermal vent copper-sulfide geochemistry — Pourbaix/Eh-pH speciation of Cu+/Cu2+ in sulfide-rich fluids, chalcopyrite/covellite precipitation, copper bioavailability windows
bridge_concepts: FDX1 ferredoxin — upstream regulator of lipoylation and Cu2+→Cu+ reduction
DLAT lipoylation pathway (LIAS, LIPT1, DLD) — Cu2+ binds lipoyl moiety causing oligomerization
Cu+/Cu2+ speciation via Pourbaix/Eh-pH diagrams — quantitative framework from geochemistry
Chalcopyrite (CuFeS2) and covellite (CuS) — defined Cu oxidation states in mineral phases
Copper chaperones (CCS, ATOX1, COX17) — characterized independently in both domains
Elesclomol copper ionophore — delivers Cu2+ to mitochondria, speciation determines efficacy
scout_targets.rationale: Cuproptosis research treats copper as a single variable but geochemistry shows Cu speciation (Cu+/Cu2+, free vs complexed, sulfide-bound) determines reactivity. FDX1-dependent Cu2+ reduction is an Eh transition geochemists model routinely. Pourbaix diagrams applied to mitochondrial Eh (-250 to -300 mV) predict which Cu species trigger DLAT oligomerization. Mirrors successful ferroptosis × serpentinization pattern (S005: 43% QG pass). Indirect enzymatic cascade with named molecules (~100% survival bridge type).

scout_targets.field_a: Coral calcification biomineralization — CARP/galaxin organic matrix control, STPCA carbonic anhydrase, calicoblastic pH regulation of aragonite deposition
scout_targets.field_c: Vascular calcification in atherosclerosis — smooth muscle osteogenic transdifferentiation, MGP/fetuin-A inhibitor failure, hydroxyapatite nucleation in arterial walls
bridge_concepts: Carbonic anhydrase isoforms (STPCA in coral calicoblasts ↔ CA-II/CA-IX in vascular smooth muscle)
pH regulation at calcification front (coral proton pumps ↔ vascular lesion microenvironment)
BMP-2 osteogenic signaling (conserved across metazoa, drives vascular transdifferentiation)
SLC4/SLC26 bicarbonate transporters (essential for coral CaCO3, unexplored in vascular calcification)
scout_targets.rationale: Coral solved controlled biomineralization; arteries lose this control in disease. Despite different mineral phases (CaCO3 vs hydroxyapatite), both use carbonic anhydrase, acid-rich matrix proteins, and proton pumps. Coral's calcifying fluid pH measurement (8.5-9.0 maintained by proton pumping) offers quantitative model for vascular lesion pH — an unmeasured variable in atherosclerosis. CARPs structural analogues of osteopontin aspartate-rich domains suggest shared inhibitory mechanisms.

scout_targets.field_a: Biofilm matrix polysaccharide mechanics — Psl/Pel/alginate matrices in P. aeruginosa, basic shear rheology and microindentation characterization
scout_targets.field_c: Cartilage extracellular matrix biomechanics — 45 years of biphasic/triphasic theory, Donnan equilibrium, aggregate modulus, hydraulic permeability for charged polysaccharide networks
bridge_concepts: Biphasic theory (Mow 1980) — solid matrix + interstitial fluid framework, never applied to biofilms
Donnan equilibrium / fixed charge density (sulfated GAGs in cartilage ↔ charged Psl/alginate in biofilm)
Aggregate modulus (Ha) — standard cartilage parameter, unmeasured in biofilm literature
Hydraulic permeability (k) — controls solute transport, measured in cartilage, unmeasured in biofilms
Osmotic swelling pressure — drives compressive resistance via charge, applicable to biofilm hydration
Enzymatic degradation parallels (MMPs/ADAMTS ↔ alginate lyase/dispersin B)
scout_targets.rationale: Biofilm mechanics is a rapidly growing field (2020-2025) that explicitly lacks predictive quantitative frameworks. Cartilage biomechanics provides 45 years of continuum mechanics for physically analogous systems (charged polysaccharide networks with interstitial fluid). Applying biphasic theory to biofilms predicts: fixed charge density determines antibiotic penetration depth via permeability-charge coupling. Published dimensional mismatch — aggregate modulus, hydraulic permeability, and osmotic swelling pressure are measured in cartilage but unmeasured in biofilms despite identical physics.
